caveats: a: The occupation source is four-digit NAICS 4859, not six-digit 485999.
a: Employment shares are only a bridge to wage-weighted task exposure.
a: The frozen compensation-to-receipts ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to the IPS basis as stated in the assignment.
rho: No source directly measures five-year implementation of AI labor substitution in private NAICS 485999 firms.
rho: Autonomous driving is not assumed to contribute materially to implementation within the base case.
rho: Regular-route shuttles and demand-responsive vanpools have different workflow complexity.
e: The NAICS residual category combines airport and hotel shuttles, carpools, vanpools, and other passenger transport.
e: No source identifies which LMM firms meet the transferable outsourced-service lens.
e: The frozen firm count is a margin-bridged estimate rather than observed EBITDA-band firm data.
s5: Gallup covers US employer businesses across industries and sizes.
s5: Plans include gifts and rare public offerings and are not completed control transfers.
s5: Closures and internal reorganizations do not qualify.
q: No source directly measures automation-savings pass-through in this industry.
q: Capture differs among public vanpool programs, private passenger fares, parking shuttles, and fixed-fee institutional contracts.
q: Demand effects and implementation difficulty are excluded from this primitive.
d5: Neither proxy measures constant-price, constant-quality demand for NAICS 485999.
d5: The residual industry has heterogeneous demand drivers and customer concentration.
d5: Employment projections embed productivity and occupational demand, not only service quantity.
o: No source measures the five-year operator-required share for the target lens.
o: Autonomous-shuttle readiness can change nonlinearly with regulation and vehicle capability.
o: The accountable operator may use remote supervision or rider-drivers rather than today's staffing model.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat passenger fares, monthly vanpool payments, route or service contracts with airports, hotels, parking operators, employers, campuses, and transit sponsors, and in some cases public operating or capital support.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive hotel, airport, campus, or employer fleets; urban transit systems; taxis and ride-hail; limousine, school-bus, charter-bus, special-needs, and ambulance services; pure software platforms; and one-off event movements without repeat-service economics.
lens.included_activities: Private lower-middle-market operators of recurring or repeat shuttle, airport-transfer, hotel-transfer, carpool, vanpool, and other scheduled ground-passenger services for external customers or contracting organizations.

a.bridge: May 2023 BLS OEWS measures broader NAICS 4859, where transportation occupations are 74.40% of employment, passenger-vehicle drivers 64.64%, and office/administrative support 15.10%. I expose portions of reservations, customer communications, scheduling, dispatch, route planning, reconciliation, and supervision while leaving most current driving, boarding, inspection, cleaning, and maintenance tasks unexposed. Because 4859 also includes special-needs transportation and the target code may have more regular-route shuttles, the error direction is uncertain.
caveats: The occupation source is four-digit NAICS 4859, not six-digit 485999.
Employment shares are only a bridge to wage-weighted task exposure.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to the IPS basis as stated in the assignment.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time study across airport shuttles, hotel shuttles, parking shuttles, and administered vanpools, with separate payroll for drivers, dispatch, reservations, sales, billing, and maintenance, would materially move the range.
a.rationale: Regular routes limit some dispatch complexity, but operators still handle bookings, manifests, flight or shift changes, vehicle assignments, service alerts, driver communications, invoices, and customer support. These workflows are amenable to AI assistance; most payroll remains attached to moving and maintaining vehicles.

d5.bridge: BLS projects shuttle-driver and chauffeur employment to rise 6.7% from 2024 to 2034, and BTS reported December 2024 US airline scheduled passengers at a new all-time high, up 5.9% year over year. I use these broad indicators to anchor modest five-year real quantity growth, tempered by telework exposure in vanpools, hotel and airport cycles, parking-mode changes, and productivity. Occupation-wide employment includes other industries and airline passengers do not translate one-for-one into shuttle trips, so the error direction is uncertain.
caveats: Neither proxy measures constant-price, constant-quality demand for NAICS 485999.
The residual industry has heterogeneous demand drivers and customer concentration.
Employment projections embed productivity and occupational demand, not only service quantity.
d5.change_evidence: A revenue-weighted activity forecast using airport ground-access trips, hotel shuttle boardings, parking-shuttle trips, vanpool passenger miles, route starts and closures, and telework incidence would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: Airport and visitor flows support shuttle demand, while repeat commuter and institutional routes provide a recurring base. Telework, customer insourcing, ride-hail, rental-car and parking changes, transit links, and contract losses can shrink specific operators even if aggregate passenger flows rise.

caveats: The NAICS residual category combines airport and hotel shuttles, carpools, vanpools, and other passenger transport.
No source identifies which LMM firms meet the transferable outsourced-service lens.
The frozen firm count is a margin-bridged estimate rather than observed EBITDA-band firm data.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level classification of the frozen 66 LMM-band firms by activity, ownership, recurring-revenue share, top-customer concentration, concession duration, fleet ownership, and owner dependence would directly measure eligibility.
e.rationale: Many scaled operators deliver recurring outsourced routes or administer repeat vanpool payments and therefore fit the lens. Eligibility is reduced by captive fleets, public or subsidy-dependent structures without transferable control economics, businesses reliant on a single concession, weak fleet condition, and owner-dependent firms.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 485999 ($2,063,993,669) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 485999 ($2,988,980,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0983 (margins.json[48]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Transportation EBITDA/Sales 9.83%); revenue/employee $129,899 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 485999); class <5: 1,338 firms, avg revenue/firm $608,267 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $59,793 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 237 firms, avg revenue/firm $977,717 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $96,110 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 143 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,537,007 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $151,088 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 129 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,153,349 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $309,974 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 41 firms, avg revenue/firm $15,962,098 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,569,074 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 25 firms, avg revenue/firm $26,495,200 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,604,478 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 66

caveats: No source measures the five-year operator-required share for the target lens.
Autonomous-shuttle readiness can change nonlinearly with regulation and vehicle capability.
The accountable operator may use remote supervision or rider-drivers rather than today's staffing model.
o.change_evidence: Commercial deployment records showing paid driverless shuttle miles, routes without onboard attendants, remote-operator ratios, regulatory approvals, intervention rates, and substitution from managed vanpools to self-organized carpools would directly test this estimate.
o.rationale: Most remaining passenger trips still need a fleet operator accountable for vehicles, safety, routes, accessibility, disruptions, and customer contracts. Software can self-serve booking and coordination, and some vanpools can be rider-driven, but federal automated-shuttle evidence still shows limited operating domains, onboard attendants, and technology not ready for mainstream scale.

caveats: No source directly measures automation-savings pass-through in this industry.
Capture differs among public vanpool programs, private passenger fares, parking shuttles, and fixed-fee institutional contracts.
Demand effects and implementation difficulty are excluded from this primitive.
q.change_evidence: Contract and route cohorts showing pricing, renewal bids, service-level penalties, utilization, gross margins, and fare changes before and after workflow automation would materially tighten the retained-benefit estimate.
q.rationale: Fixed route-service contracts and posted fares can preserve savings until renewal, and better on-time performance can differentiate operators. Over five years, competitive concessions, hotel or employer procurement, public subsidies, fare competition, and contract rebids share some benefits with customers, while direct passenger routes may retain more.

caveats: No source directly measures five-year implementation of AI labor substitution in private NAICS 485999 firms.
Autonomous driving is not assumed to contribute materially to implementation within the base case.
Regular-route shuttles and demand-responsive vanpools have different workflow complexity.
rho.change_evidence: Matched before-and-after deployment data from private shuttle and vanpool operators covering administrative hours per trip, call handling, schedule interventions, utilization, missed trips, complaints, and billing errors would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Booking, communications, schedule maintenance, dispatch support, reconciliation, and reporting can be integrated over five years using current software and AI assistants. Real-time disruptions, fragmented customer systems, safety oversight, legacy fleet technology, union or workforce rules, and thin LMM implementation capacity keep realization well below the full exposed opportunity.

s5.bridge: Gallup's fall-2024 US survey found that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. I use the employer-business rate as the anchor for eligible LMM shuttle and vanpool firms, widening for industry-specific ownership age, customer concentration, fleet capital needs, and the gap between intent and closing. Stated plans can overstate completed qualifying transfers, while unplanned transactions can partly offset that bias; net direction is uncertain.
caveats: Gallup covers US employer businesses across industries and sizes.
Plans include gifts and rare public offerings and are not completed control transfers.
Closures and internal reorganizations do not qualify.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year dataset of closed and failed NAICS 485999 business sales, including asset sales, internal transfers, sponsor acquisitions, closures, seller age, and concession assignment outcomes, would materially move the estimate.
s5.rationale: Employer businesses are the closest directly observed transfer-planning population. A shuttle business can transfer if route contracts, permits, fleet, systems, and managers survive the owner, but concession consent and customer concentration can make intended sales fail or become asset-only wind-downs.

principal_driver: Repeat route operations create automatable coordination work while airport and institutional passenger flows sustain physical service demand.
principal_weakness: Driver-heavy delivery and concentrated route contracts constrain both labor substitution and long-run retention of savings.

run_meta.title: All Other Transit and Ground Passenger Transportation

sources.supports: Defines the industry to include shuttle services and car pools or vanpools, with shuttles generally operating regular routes and schedules between hotels, airports, or other destinations; reports 2,285 employer establishments in 2023 County Business Patterns.
sources.title: 485999: All Other Transit and Ground Passenger Transportation — Census Bureau Profile

sources.supports: Broader NAICS 4859 occupation mix: transportation occupations 74.40%, passenger-vehicle drivers 64.64%, shuttle drivers and chauffeurs 52.75%, and office/administrative support 15.10% of employment.
sources.title: Other Transit and Ground Passenger Transportation — May 2023 OEWS Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates

sources.supports: Fall-2024 US survey found that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, versus 9% of nonemployers.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Defines private public-transportation vanpool providers and commuter vanpool vehicles, including the expectation that at least 80% of mileage is for home-to-work commuter transport.
sources.title: FTA Van Pool Policy — FAQs

sources.supports: BLS projects shuttle-driver and chauffeur employment from 243,900 in 2024 to 260,300 in 2034, an increase of 16,400 or 6.7%.
sources.title: Occupational projections and worker characteristics, 2024–2034

sources.supports: BTS reported 83.3 million systemwide scheduled-service passengers in December 2024 and a seasonally adjusted all-time high, providing a directional airport-shuttle demand indicator.
sources.title: December 2024 U.S. Airline Traffic Data Up 5.9% from December 2023

sources.supports: NCDOT pilot lessons state automated-shuttle technology was not ready for mainstream scale, had restricted compliance and operating conditions, and required an onboard attendant.
sources.title: Future automated shuttle deployments should incorporate route design features such as dedicated lanes and simplified operating environments

title: All Other Transit and Ground Passenger Transportation
